ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general uttapam entry (FOODON_03311069); no specific vegetable-topped variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific uttapam code. 'Pancake' (A0CKP) is the closest defensible parent category for this fermented rice-lentil pancake.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for uttapam or this specific South Indian fermented pancake preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vegetable uthappam consists of two medium-sized pancakes totalling approximately 200 g, served with coconut chutney and sambar.

nutrient_source.title: Uttapam (Vegetable) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for plain uttapam (fermented rice-lentil pancake). Vegetable-topped variant is adapted by incorporating typical vegetable additions (onion, tomato, green chilli, coriander) at approximately 20% by weight of the finished product. Macronutrient values adjusted proportionally. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with ICMR-NIN plain uttapam baseline adjusted for vegetable content.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of South Indian fermented foods: uttapam and related preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for fermented South Indian rice-lentil preparations. Plain uttapam has a reported GI of approximately 51–53 (low category) based on fermented rice-lentil batter studies. Vegetable uthappam is estimated at GI 51 (low) as the addition of vegetables does not materially increase the glycemic response and may modestly lower it due to added dietary fibre. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (51 × 25.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 26.0 g.

hypertension.guidance: Vegetable uthappam contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (620 mg per typical 200 g serving), which represents about 27% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as salted chutneys or pickles.

type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low glycemic index of 51 and a glycemic load of approximately 26 per standard serving, vegetable uthappam produces a moderate postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume one piece rather than two and pair with high-fibre sambar to further blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and cholesterol-free profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetable uthappam is cholesterol-free with low saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and is prepared with minimal oil on a griddle, making it a heart-friendly choice; however, sodium intake from the dish and its accompaniments should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Uthappam (also spelled Uttapam) is a thick, soft South Indian pancake made from a fermented batter of rice and urad dal (black gram lentils), topped with finely chopped vegetables such as onion, tomato, green chilli, and fresh coriander, which are pressed into the batter during cooking on a lightly oiled griddle or tawa. Unlike dosa, which is thin and crispy, uthappam is thick and spongy with a slightly sour flavour from fermentation, and the embedded vegetables add sweetness, crunch, and colour. It is a staple breakfast and tiffin item across Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Kerala, and is widely served in South Indian restaurants throughout Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, vegetable uthappam provides moderate energy (approximately 148 kcal per 100 g), a useful combination of complex carbohydrates from rice and plant protein from urad dal, dietary fibre from both the lentils and vegetables, and is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and cholesterol-free. The fermentation process enhances bioavailability of B vitamins and improves digestibility. Its low glycemic index of approximately 51 makes it a more suitable carbohydrate choice for individuals managing blood glucose compared to refined-grain alternatives. Sodium content is moderate and should be considered when served with salted accompaniments. It is typically served with coconut chutney and sambar.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain uttapam data with proportional adjustment for vegetable content. No exact entry for vegetable uthappam exists in ICMR-NIN; adaptation is methodologically sound but introduces minor uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fermented rice-lentil preparation data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) plain uttapam entry, adjusted for approximately 20% vegetable content by weight. 2. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as this is a plant-based preparation with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a vegan preparation containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero. 8. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based fermented food with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero. 9. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for fermented South Indian rice-lentil preparations; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 10. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (51 × 25.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 26.0 g for a 200 g serving. 11. FoodOn match type is closest_parent as no specific vegetable uthappam entry exists. 12. FoodEx2 match type is closest_parent using Pancake (A0CKP) as the nearest defensible category. 13. SNOMED CT match type is none as no suitable code exists for this preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Populated all mandatory multilingual local_names for Indian/Singaporean cuisine: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Adapted ICMR-NIN plain uttapam nutrient values for vegetable-topped variant with documented methodology.
Set GI to 51 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for fermented South Indian rice-lentil preparations.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-not-reported justification in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg) with confirmed-zero justification in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent — all language-specific strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Set ontology match types to only allowed values: exact, closest_parent, or none.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three required health conditions.
